title: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer
authors: Susan J. Mandel, Anthony P. Weetman, J. Larry Jameson

items.title: Why TSH is the first test for a thyroid nodule
items.content:
Evaluation of any thyroid nodule begins with serum TSH because the result branches the entire workup:
• If TSH is SUPPRESSED → get a radionuclide scan. A 'hot' (autonomously functioning) nodule is almost never malignant, so FNA is unnecessary — you treat the hyperthyroidism instead.
• If TSH is normal or high → proceed to ultrasound to characterize the nodule (pattern + size) and decide on FNA.

This single test prevents needless biopsy of benign hot nodules and directs the rest of the algorithm.
keyPoints: Suppressed TSH → scan; hot nodule ≈ benign, no FNA.
Normal/high TSH → ultrasound and risk-stratify.
Most patients with nodules are euthyroid.
items.reference: Approach to Thyroid Nodules: "If the TSH is suppressed, a radionuclide scan is indicated to determine if the identified nodule is 'hot,' as lesions with increased uptake are almost never malignant and FNA is unnecessary."

items.title: Ultrasound risk stratification (TI-RADS) and FNA size cutoffs
items.content:
Ultrasound pattern + nodule size drive the FNA decision via validated risk-stratification systems (ACR TI-RADS most used in the US; also ATA, EU-TIRADS). Higher-suspicion patterns are biopsied at SMALLER sizes.

• Spongiform (TR1): <3% cancer → observe, no FNA.
• Solid hypoechoic, smooth border (TR4): 10–20% malignant → FNA at ~1–1.5 cm.
• Highest-suspicion (TR5): FNA at 1 cm.
• No routine FNA for any nodule <1 cm unless metastatic cervical nodes are present.
These systems cut unnecessary FNAs by ≥45%.
keyPoints: Higher sonographic suspicion → biopsy at smaller size.
Spongiform = essentially benign; don't FNA.
Sub-centimeter nodules: don't FNA unless nodal metastases.
items.reference: Approach: "a spongiform nodule (TR1)... has a <3% chance of cancer... All the RSSs recommend FNA at 1 cm for the highest suspicion pattern nodule, TR5... none of the RSSs recommend routine FNA for any nodule <1 cm unless metastatic cervical lymph nodes are present."

items.title: Why FNA cannot diagnose follicular carcinoma
items.content:
FNA is excellent for papillary thyroid cancer because PTC has diagnostic NUCLEAR features (clear nuclei, grooves, 'Orphan Annie eye'). But follicular adenoma and follicular carcinoma share identical nuclear cytology — the malignant diagnosis depends on CAPSULAR and/or VASCULAR invasion, which can only be seen on histology of the whole excised nodule.

Consequence: a follicular lesion on FNA is 'indeterminate.' Definitive diagnosis requires surgery (diagnostic lobectomy) or molecular testing to refine cancer risk and avoid unnecessary operations.
keyPoints: FNA diagnoses PTC well; cannot confirm/exclude follicular carcinoma.
Follicular carcinoma = capsular/vascular invasion (histology only).
Indeterminate cytology → molecular testing or diagnostic lobectomy.
items.reference: WDTC/Approach: "follicular carcinoma is diagnosed by the presence of capsular and/or vascular invasion... the distinction between benign and malignant follicular patterned lesions is often not possible using cytology alone."

items.title: The Bethesda system and indeterminate cytology
items.content:
FNA cytology is reported with the six-tier Bethesda system, each tier carrying an estimated malignancy risk. Typical yields: ~50–60% benign, ~5% malignant/suspicious, ~5–7% nondiagnostic, and 25–40% INDETERMINATE.

Indeterminate splits into: AUS (atypia of undetermined significance), FN (follicular neoplasm), and SFM (suspicious for malignancy). AUS/FN are mostly benign (malignancy risk ~13–34%) — historically driving many diagnostic lobectomies for benign disease, which is why molecular testing was developed to safely de-escalate.
keyPoints: Six tiers, each with a malignancy probability.
Indeterminate = AUS, FN, SFM.
Molecular testing reduces surgery for benign indeterminate nodules.
items.reference: Approach: "The Bethesda System... This six-tiered classification system... AUS... FN... and SFM... the majority of nodules with AUS and FN cytology results are benign; the range of malignancy (ROM) varies from 13 to 34%."

items.title: Why thyroid cancer is considered 'overdiagnosed'
items.content:
Incidence rose from ~4.9 to >15/100,000 over 30 years, but disease-specific mortality barely changed. Three conditions define overdiagnosis and all are met: (1) a large disease reservoir (autopsy PTC prevalence ~25%); (2) detection activity (more imaging incidentally finding small nodules); and (3) a diagnosis-vs-mortality mismatch (mortality flat for 40 years).

Clinical response: higher size thresholds for FNA, active surveillance for micropapillary cancers, and less aggressive surgery/radioiodine for low-risk tumors. US diagnoses have since fallen (62,450 in 2015 → 43,720 in 2023).
keyPoints: Rising incidence, flat mortality = overdiagnosis of indolent small PTC.
20-year mortality of low-risk thyroid cancer is ~1%.
Guidelines now limit FNA and de-escalate low-risk treatment.
items.reference: THYROID CANCER: "the autopsy prevalence of PTC is ~25%... thyroid cancer disease-specific mortality not changed in 40 years... The 20-year disease-specific mortality for low-risk thyroid cancer is 1%."

items.title: Papillary thyroid cancer: features and spread
items.content:
PTC is the most common thyroid cancer (80–85% of differentiated tumors). Diagnostic cytology: large clear nuclei with 'Orphan Annie eye' chromatin, nuclear grooves, prominent nucleoli. It is often multifocal, spreads mainly via LYMPHATICS (but can go hematogenously to lung/bone), and usually has an excellent prognosis (>95% stage I/II).

Nodal nuance: micrometastases (<2 mm) do not affect prognosis, but gross involvement of multiple 2–3 cm nodes implies 25–30% recurrence and can increase mortality in older patients. Aggressive variants (tall cell, columnar, hobnail, poorly differentiated) need more intensive therapy.
keyPoints: Orphan Annie nuclei + grooves; lymphatic spread; often multifocal.
Micromets <2 mm = no prognostic impact; bulky nodal disease matters.
Aggressive variants: tall cell, columnar, hobnail.
items.reference: WDTC: "Orphan Annie eye... nuclear grooves... Micrometastases, defined as <2 mm of cancer in a lymph node, do not affect prognosis. However, gross metastatic involvement of multiple 2- to 3-cm lymph nodes indicates a 25–30% chance of recurrence."

items.title: Why follicular carcinoma behaves differently from papillary
items.content:
FTC (~5% of US thyroid cancers, commoner in iodine-deficient areas) spreads HEMATOGENOUSLY (bone, lung, CNS) rather than via lymphatics. Its malignant nature is defined by capsular/vascular invasion seen only on histology.

Risk stratifies by invasion: capsular-invasion-only FTC has very low metastatic risk and lobectomy suffices; angioinvasive FTC is aggressive. Poor prognostic features: distant metastases, age >55, primary tumor >4 cm, and marked vascular invasion.
keyPoints: Hematogenous spread (bone/lung/CNS), not lymphatic.
Capsular-only invasion → lobectomy; angioinvasion → aggressive.
Poor prognosis: mets, >55 y, >4 cm, marked vascular invasion.
items.reference: WDTC (Follicular): "Follicular carcinomas with only capsular invasion have a very low risk of metastasis, and lobectomy alone suffices. Angioinvasive FTC is more aggressive and may metastasize to bone, lung, and the central nervous system."

items.title: Why the MAPK pathway is central to papillary cancer
items.content:
Up to 70% of PTCs activate the RET-RAS-BRAF (MAPK) signaling cascade. BRAF V600E (up to 60% of PTC) and RET/PTC rearrangements (15%; increased after Chernobyl) and RAS mutations all converge on MAPK. Crucially, BRAF, RAS, and RET mutations are mutually exclusive in the same tumor — implying MAPK activation is the common driver, regardless of which step triggers it.

Clinical relevance: this pathway is the target of mutation-selective kinase inhibitors (e.g., BRAF V600E inhibitors) and of differentiation strategies aiming to restore radioiodine uptake in refractory disease.
keyPoints: BRAF/RAS/RET converge on MAPK; mutations are mutually exclusive.
RET/PTC rearrangements rose after Chernobyl (radiation).
MAPK targeting underlies modern kinase-inhibitor therapy.
items.reference: Genetics: "Activation of the RET-RAS-BRAF signaling pathway is seen in up to 70% of PTCs... BRAF V600E mutations... occurring in up to 60% of PTC... simultaneous RET, BRAF, and RAS mutations rarely occur in the same tumor."

items.title: Why every MTC patient needs RET testing and a pheochromocytoma check
items.content:
Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC, ~5% of thyroid cancers) arises from calcitonin-producing C cells. It can be sporadic or familial (MEN 2A, MEN 2B, familial MTC), and familial forms carry germline RET point mutations. Therefore ALL patients with MTC should be tested for RET, so that mutation-positive relatives can be screened.

Before any surgery in a RET-mutation patient, pheochromocytoma MUST be excluded — operating on an unrecognized pheochromocytoma risks a fatal hypertensive crisis. MTC does not take up radioiodine; calcitonin is the tumor marker, and management is primarily surgical.
keyPoints: Test all MTC for RET (familial MEN 2 implications).
Exclude pheochromocytoma before surgery in RET-mutation carriers.
MTC: calcitonin marker; no radioiodine uptake; surgery-based.
items.reference: MTC: "All patients with MTC should be tested for RET mutations... Prior to surgery, pheochromocytoma should be excluded in all patients with a RET mutation... these tumors do not take up radioiodine."

items.title: Anaplastic carcinoma and thyroid lymphoma: rapidly growing masses
items.content:
A rapidly enlarging thyroid mass is an alarm for two very different aggressive entities:
• Anaplastic thyroid cancer (ATC): poorly differentiated, most patients die within 6 months; negligible radioiodine uptake; chemotherapy usually ineffective; characteristic mutations include TERT, BRAF, CTNNB1 (β-catenin) and P53.
• Thyroid lymphoma: usually diffuse large B-cell lymphoma arising on a background of Hashimoto's thyroiditis; highly radiosensitive. SURGICAL resection should be avoided as initial therapy (may spread otherwise-localized disease) — treat as lymphoma.
keyPoints: Rapidly growing mass → think ATC or lymphoma.
ATC: dismal prognosis, no radioiodine uptake.
Lymphoma (DLBCL on Hashimoto's): radiosensitive — do NOT resect first.
items.reference: ANAPLASTIC/Lymphoma: "most patients die within 6 months of diagnosis... Lymphoma in the thyroid gland often arises in the background of Hashimoto's thyroiditis... Surgical resection should be avoided as initial therapy because it may spread disease."

items.title: Why TSH suppression is part of thyroid cancer therapy
items.content:
Most differentiated thyroid cancers still express TSH receptors and remain TSH-responsive; higher TSH (even within the normal range) is associated with increased cancer risk. Suppressing TSH with levothyroxine therefore removes a growth stimulus to residual tumor.

Degree is risk-tailored: low-risk → TSH 0.5–2.0; intermediate → 0.1–0.5; high-risk or known metastatic disease → <0.1 mIU/L (accepting mild thyrotoxicosis if not contraindicated). Suppression is relaxed over time as surveillance confirms remission.
keyPoints: Tumors are TSH-responsive → suppress TSH to limit growth.
Target depends on recurrence risk (0.5–2.0 low; <0.1 high/metastatic).
De-escalate suppression once disease-free.
items.reference: TSH SUPPRESSION: "Because most tumors are still TSH-responsive, LT4 suppression of TSH has been mainstay of thyroid cancer treatment... For patients at low risk... 0.5–2.0 mIU/L... high risk... <0.1 mIU/L."

items.title: Why high TSH and low iodine are required before radioiodine therapy
items.content:
Radioiodine uptake into thyroid remnant/tumor depends on the sodium-iodide symporter (NIS), which is STIMULATED BY TSH (via the TSH receptor). So uptake — and therefore treatment efficacy — is maximized by raising TSH to >25 mIU/L, achieved either by thyroid-hormone withdrawal (endogenous TSH rise) or recombinant human TSH (rhTSH) injections. A 1–2 week low-iodine diet depletes the competing stable-iodine pool so the radioactive iodine is preferentially taken up.

This also explains why radioablation works best after near-total thyroidectomy: minimal remnant means the dose concentrates where it's needed and enables Tg surveillance.
keyPoints: TSH drives NIS-mediated iodine uptake → need TSH >25 (withdrawal or rhTSH).
Low-iodine diet removes competing stable iodine.
Works best with minimal thyroid remnant (post near-total thyroidectomy).
items.reference: RADIOIODINE: "Radioiodine uptake is determined primarily by expression of the NIS and is stimulated by TSH... ideally, the serum TSH level is >25 mIU/L at the time of 131I therapy... the patient follows a low-iodine diet for 1–2 weeks."

items.title: Why thyroglobulin works as a cancer surveillance marker
items.content:
After total thyroidectomy and remnant radioablation, essentially no normal thyroid tissue remains to make thyroglobulin (Tg). Therefore any detectable/ rising Tg signals residual or recurrent differentiated thyroid cancer — a sensitive biochemical marker (modern assays detect to 0.1 ng/mL).

Because most PTC recurrences are in cervical nodes, neck ultrasound at ~6 months is more sensitive than whole-body scanning for nodal recurrence. Low-risk patients with negative neck US and basal Tg <0.2 ng/mL have <3% structural recurrence at 5 years and can have follow-up de-escalated.
keyPoints: Post-ablation, Tg should be near-undetectable → rising Tg = recurrence.
Neck ultrasound > whole-body scan for nodal recurrence.
Negative US + Tg <0.2 → <3% recurrence; relax surveillance.
items.reference: Surveillance: "Serum thyroglobulin (Tg) is a sensitive marker of residual/recurrent thyroid cancer after ablation... a neck ultrasound should be performed about 6 months after thyroid ablation; ultrasound has been shown to be more sensitive than WBS."

items.title: How a toxic adenoma becomes autonomous
items.content:
Most solitary hyperfunctioning ('toxic') adenomas carry acquired somatic ACTIVATING mutations in the TSH receptor (less often in Gsα). These mutations make the receptor constitutively couple to Gsα, raising cyclic AMP and driving follicular-cell proliferation and hormone production INDEPENDENT of TSH — so the nodule keeps working even though pituitary TSH is suppressed. >90% of solitary hyperfunctioning nodules have such mutations.

Clinical correlates: a thyroid scan shows focal uptake in the nodule with suppression of the rest of the gland; thyrotoxicosis usually appears only when the nodule exceeds ~3 cm. Hot nodules are almost never malignant.
keyPoints: Constitutively active TSH-R (or Gsα) mutation → autonomous function.
Scan: focal 'hot' nodule, suppressed background gland.
Hot nodule ≈ benign; thyrotoxicosis usually when >3 cm.
items.reference: Hyperfunctioning Solitary Nodule: "Most patients with solitary hyperfunctioning nodules have acquired somatic, activating mutations in the TSH-R... induce constitutive receptor coupling to Gsα... identified in >90% of patients."

items.title: Why radioiodine is ideal for a toxic adenoma
items.content:
Because the autonomous nodule suppresses the rest of the gland, normal thyroid tissue takes up little iodine. Radioiodine therefore concentrates in the hyperfunctioning nodule with minimal uptake/damage to normal tissue — making 131I the treatment of choice. Relatively large doses correct thyrotoxicosis in ~75% within 3 months; hypothyroidism develops in <10% over 5 years.

Alternatives: lobectomy (preserves function, low risk of hypoparathyroidism/RLN injury) or antithyroid drugs/β-blockers (effective but not optimal long term). Unlike Graves', spontaneous remission does not occur.
keyPoints: Suppressed normal gland → 131I selectively hits the nodule.
~75% corrected by 3 months; <10% later hypothyroidism.
No spontaneous remission (unlike Graves').
items.reference: TREATMENT (Hyperfunctioning Solitary Nodule): "Because normal thyroid function is suppressed, 131I is concentrated in the hyperfunctioning nodule with minimal uptake and damage to normal thyroid tissue... correct thyrotoxicosis in ~75% of patients within 3 months."

items.title: Why iodine exposure is dangerous in nodular goiter
items.content:
Autonomous regions in a multinodular goiter make hormone independent of TSH. Giving an iodine load (e.g., CT contrast, amiodarone) supplies extra substrate that these autonomous nodules convert into excess thyroid hormone — the Jod-Basedow phenomenon — precipitating or worsening thyrotoxicosis.

Practical rule: avoid iodinated contrast and other iodine sources in patients with nodular goiter/autonomy. In toxic MNG the TSH is low, T3 is often elevated more than T4, and the scan shows patchy heterogeneous uptake.
keyPoints: Iodine load + autonomous nodules → Jod-Basedow thyrotoxicosis.
Avoid iodinated contrast in nodular goiter.
Toxic MNG: low TSH, T3>T4, heterogeneous uptake.
items.reference: Nontoxic MNG TREATMENT: "Contrast agents and other iodine-containing substances should be avoided because of the risk of inducing the Jod-Basedow effect, characterized by enhanced thyroid hormone production by autonomous nodules."

items.title: Why iodine deficiency causes goiter
items.content:
Iodine deficiency reduces the efficiency of thyroid hormone synthesis. The resulting (often only slightly) increased TSH — plus increased TSH sensitivity and local growth factors — stimulates compensatory thyroid growth to trap more iodide and maintain hormone output. Worldwide, iodine deficiency is the most common cause of diffuse goiter (endemic when >5% of a population is affected).

Other causes: environmental goitrogens (cassava root — a thiocyanate source; cruciferous vegetables) and rare inherited dyshormonogenesis (defects in NIS, Tg, TPO, dehalogenase).
keyPoints: Inefficient synthesis → ↑TSH/growth signals → compensatory goiter.
Iodine deficiency is the leading cause worldwide; endemic if >5%.
Goitrogens (cassava, cruciferous vegetables) and dyshormonogenesis also cause goiter.
items.reference: Diffuse Nontoxic Goiter: "diffuse goiter is most commonly caused by iodine deficiency and is termed endemic goiter when it affects >5% of the population... thyroid enlargement reflects a compensatory effort to trap iodide."

items.title: Pemberton's sign and assessing a substernal goiter
items.content:
Pemberton's sign = facial/neck congestion (suffusion) when the patient raises both arms above the head — the maneuver pulls a large/substernal goiter into the thoracic inlet, obstructing jugular venous return. It signals significant thoracic-inlet compression.

Workup of obstructive goiter: respiratory-flow measurements (compression characteristically causes inspiratory stridor) and CT/MRI to map substernal extent and tracheal narrowing; barium swallow for esophageal compression. Note tracheal compression usually must exceed ~70% of the lumen to compromise the airway.
keyPoints: Positive Pemberton's sign = thoracic-inlet venous obstruction.
Evaluate with CT/MRI + flow-volume loops (inspiratory stridor).
Significant airway compromise usually needs >70% tracheal narrowing.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations: "Pemberton's sign refers to facial and neck congestion due to jugular venous obstruction when the arms are raised above the head... compression must usually exceed 70% of the tracheal diameter before there is significant airway compromise."

items.title: How to tell type 1 from type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis
items.content:
Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (AIT) has two mechanisms with different treatments:
• Type 1 — occurs in an abnormal gland (preclinical Graves'/nodular goiter); the iodine load drives excess synthesis (Jod-Basedow). Doppler shows INCREASED vascularity. Treat with antithyroid drugs (± potassium perchlorate to reduce thyroidal iodide).
• Type 2 — a destructive thyroiditis from a direct toxic effect on follicular cells, in a normal gland; Doppler shows DECREASED vascularity. Treat with GLUCOCORTICOIDS (prednisone 40–60 mg/d).
Some patients have mixed features. Because amiodarone is fat-stored, stopping it gives no immediate benefit; near-total thyroidectomy is the fastest definitive option when needed.
keyPoints: Type 1 = iodine-induced synthesis (abnormal gland), ↑vascularity → antithyroid drugs.
Type 2 = destructive thyroiditis (normal gland), ↓vascularity → glucocorticoids.
Stopping amiodarone gives no immediate benefit (fat storage).
items.reference: Amiodarone: "Type 1 AIT is associated with an underlying thyroid abnormality... (Jod-Basedow phenomenon)... Type 2 AIT... is the result of drug-induced... destructive thyroiditis... Color-flow Doppler... shows increased vascularity in type 1 AIT but decreased vascularity in type 2 AIT... Glucocorticoids... are effective in treating type 2 AIT."

items.title: Choosing lobectomy vs near-total thyroidectomy
items.content:
All well-differentiated cancers >1–1.5 cm should be excised. Extent of surgery is risk-based:
• Intrathyroidal 1–4 cm (T1b/T2), no metastases: lobectomy OR near-total thyroidectomy — bilateral surgery has not been shown to improve survival.
• Near-total thyroidectomy is appropriate for tumors >4 cm, extrathyroidal extension, or metastases — and it enables radioiodine ablation and Tg monitoring in high-risk patients.
• Active surveillance is an option for intrathyroidal micropapillary cancers ≤1.5 cm without nodal disease, extrathyroidal extension, or risky posterior (near-RLN) location.
keyPoints: 1–4 cm intrathyroidal: lobectomy or near-total (survival similar).
>4 cm / ETE / mets → near-total (enables RAI + Tg surveillance).
Micropapillary ≤1.5 cm low-risk: active surveillance is acceptable.
items.reference: Surgery: "the initial surgical procedure may be either a unilateral (lobectomy) or bilateral (near-total thyroidectomy)... near-total thyroidectomy is appropriate in the presence of metastases or clinical evidence of extrathyroidal invasion and for most tumors >4 cm."

items.title: Why childhood neck irradiation raises thyroid cancer risk
items.content:
Ionizing radiation causes chromosomal breaks → gene rearrangements (e.g., RET/PTC) and loss of tumor-suppressor genes. The thyroid in CHILDREN is especially susceptible, so childhood (before age 18) head/neck irradiation and radioactive-iodine fallout (e.g., Chernobyl) markedly increase later thyroid cancer risk, often multicentric and at a younger age.

Other adverse prognostic factors: age >65, male sex (lower incidence but worse prognosis), local tumor fixation, gross nodal involvement, and distant metastases.
keyPoints: Radiation → RET/PTC rearrangements; children most susceptible.
Childhood neck irradiation/fallout = strong risk factor.
Worse prognosis: >65 y, male, fixation, gross nodes, distant mets.
items.reference: Pathogenesis (Radiation): "external radiation, which predisposes to chromosomal breaks... Children seem more predisposed to the effects of radiation than adults." Risk factors: "a history of childhood (before age 18) head or neck irradiation."

items.title: NIFTP: a 'cancer' that was reclassified as non-malignant
items.content:
The encapsulated follicular variant of PTC WITHOUT capsular or vascular invasion is no longer classified as malignant; it has been renamed noninvasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features (NIFTP). This reclassification reflects its indolent behavior and supports conservative management (lobectomy without radioiodine).

Caveat: NIFTP can only be diagnosed on surgical pathology (not FNA), so it is still counted within FNA-based malignancy-risk estimates.
keyPoints: Non-invasive encapsulated follicular-variant PTC = NIFTP (not cancer).
Indolent → conservative surgery, no radioiodine.
Diagnosed only on surgical histology, not FNA.
items.reference: WDTC: "a subtype previously known as the encapsulated PTC follicular variant, without capsular or angioinvasion, is no longer considered malignant and has been renamed noninvasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features (NIFTP)."

items.title: Why ultrasound is done even for a 'palpable' nodule
items.content:
Ultrasound is performed in nodule workup (when TSH is not suppressed) for three reasons: (1) about 15% of 'palpable' nodules are not real nodules on imaging — so no further workup is needed; (2) it detects additional non-palpable nodules that may themselves warrant FNA; and (3) it characterizes the sonographic pattern which, with size, drives the FNA decision.

This is why imaging — not the finger — defines which nodules to biopsy, and why LT4 suppression (ineffective at shrinking nodules in iodine-replete people) is not used.
keyPoints: ~15% of palpable 'nodules' aren't confirmed on US.
US finds additional nodules and assigns risk pattern.
LT4 suppression doesn't shrink nodules in iodine-replete populations.
items.reference: Approach: "About 15% of 'palpable' nodules are not confirmed on imaging... Ultrasound will characterize the imaging pattern of the nodule, which, combined with the nodule's size, facilitates decision-making about FNA."

items.title: Why high-dose 131I pretreatment scans are being avoided
items.content:
A pretreatment diagnostic scan can estimate remnant size, but a 131I scanning dose can cause 'stunning' — sublethal radiation that transiently reduces the ability of thyroid/tumor cells to take up the subsequent therapeutic dose, reducing treatment efficacy.

To avoid this, clinicians increasingly use 123I (no stunning) or proceed directly to ablation, reserving pretreatment 131I scanning for cases where remnant size would change therapy or where distant metastatic disease is suspected.
keyPoints: 131I scanning dose can 'stun' cells → less uptake of therapy.
Use 123I or go straight to ablation to avoid stunning.
Post-therapy whole-body scan confirms uptake and finds mets.
items.reference: 131I Ablation: "because of concerns about radioactive 'stunning' that impairs subsequent treatment, there is a trend to avoid pretreatment scanning with 131I and use either 123I or proceed directly to ablation."

items.title: Targeted therapy for radioiodine-refractory thyroid cancer
items.content:
When differentiated thyroid cancer loses iodine avidity and progresses, kinase inhibitors targeting RAS/BRAF/RET/VEGFR/angiogenesis can stabilize disease (though only one study showed a survival benefit). Because of toxicity and need for ongoing therapy, patient selection matters — ATA advises active surveillance for asymptomatic 1–2 cm metastases, intervening as growth accelerates.

Mutation-directed options now include BRAF V600E inhibitors and NTRK/RET fusion inhibitors; immune checkpoint inhibitors help some tumors with high tumor mutation burden. Differentiation strategies (MAPK blockade) aim to restore radioiodine uptake.
keyPoints: Kinase inhibitors stabilize RAI-refractory disease (limited survival data).
Mutation-directed: BRAF V600E, NTRK/RET fusion inhibitors.
Active surveillance for small asymptomatic metastases.
items.reference: New Potential Therapies: "Kinase inhibitors target pathways... RAS, BRAF, RET, EGFR, VEGFR... new therapies target tumor-specific mutational changes including BRAF V600E point mutations and NTRK and RET gene fusions."

items.title: Why amiodarone causes hypothyroidism — and why you needn't stop it
items.content:
Amiodarone's huge iodine load can cause hypothyroidism in susceptible patients (up to 13% in iodine-replete countries like the US) — typically those who cannot escape the Wolff-Chaikoff effect, i.e., women with positive TPO antibodies (underlying autoimmune thyroiditis).

Management: it is usually UNNECESSARY to stop amiodarone — levothyroxine normalizes thyroid function. Monitor TSH (T4 is often spuriously high because amiodarone inhibits T4→T3 conversion), and expect that LT4-replaced patients on amiodarone often need a dose increase.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism in TPO-Ab-positive patients (failed Wolff-Chaikoff escape).
Treat with LT4; usually no need to stop amiodarone.
Monitor TSH; T4 may be falsely high (↓T4→T3 conversion).
items.reference: Amiodarone: "Hypothyroidism occurs in up to 13% of amiodarone-treated patients in iodine-replete countries... more common in women and individuals with positive TPO antibodies... It is usually unnecessary to discontinue amiodarone for this side effect, because LT4 can be used to normalize thyroid function."

items.question: A 1.8 cm thyroid nodule is found incidentally. What is the most appropriate FIRST test?
items.explanation: Serum TSH is the first step. If TSH is suppressed, a radionuclide scan is done (a 'hot' nodule is almost never malignant and needs no FNA). If TSH is normal/high, ultrasound characterizes the nodule to decide on FNA. Thyroglobulin has no role in initial nodule evaluation.
items.reference: Approach: "thyroid function should be assessed by measuring a TSH level... If the TSH is suppressed, a radionuclide scan is indicated."

items.question: A patient with a thyroid nodule has a SUPPRESSED TSH, and a radionuclide scan shows the nodule is 'hot' (increased uptake) with suppression of the rest of the gland. What is the appropriate next step?
options: No FNA needed — treat the hyperthyroidism (e.g., radioiodine)
items.explanation: A hot (autonomously functioning) nodule is almost never malignant, so FNA is unnecessary; management addresses the hyperthyroidism (radioiodine is usually preferred). FNA is reserved for non-functioning ('cold') nodules selected by ultrasound pattern and size.
items.reference: Approach: "lesions with increased uptake are almost never malignant and FNA is unnecessary."

items.question: Which sonographic nodule pattern has the LOWEST malignancy risk and generally should NOT undergo FNA?
options: Solid hypoechoic nodule with irregular borders (TR5)
Solid hypoechoic nodule with smooth borders (TR4)
items.explanation: A spongiform nodule (TR1) has <3% cancer risk and is observed rather than biopsied. Higher-suspicion patterns (TR4, TR5; hypoechoic, irregular borders, microcalcifications) warrant FNA at progressively smaller size cutoffs.
items.reference: Approach: "a spongiform nodule (TR1)... has a <3% chance of cancer, and observation rather than FNA is generally recommended."

items.question: FNA of a thyroid nodule shows a follicular neoplasm. Why can't this cytology distinguish a benign follicular adenoma from follicular carcinoma?
options: Because FNA cannot sample follicular cells
Because the malignant diagnosis requires demonstrating capsular and/or vascular invasion on histology
Because follicular carcinoma has no nuclear atypia ever
items.explanation: Follicular adenoma and carcinoma share identical nuclear cytology; malignancy is defined by capsular and/or vascular invasion, which can only be assessed on histology of the resected nodule. Hence follicular lesions are 'indeterminate' on FNA, requiring molecular testing or diagnostic lobectomy.
items.reference: WDTC: "follicular carcinoma is diagnosed by the presence of capsular and/or vascular invasion because the nuclear features of follicular adenomas and carcinomas do not differ."

items.question: A patient is diagnosed with medullary thyroid carcinoma and found to carry a RET mutation. What must be done BEFORE thyroid surgery?
items.explanation: MTC with a RET mutation may be part of MEN 2, which includes pheochromocytoma. Pheochromocytoma must be excluded before surgery, because operating on an unrecognized pheochromocytoma can precipitate a fatal hypertensive crisis. MTC does not take up radioiodine, and calcitonin (not thyroglobulin) is its marker.
items.reference: MTC: "Prior to surgery, pheochromocytoma should be excluded in all patients with a RET mutation."

items.question: Which serum marker is used to monitor for residual or recurrent medullary thyroid carcinoma?
items.explanation: MTC arises from calcitonin-producing C cells, so elevated calcitonin marks residual/recurrent disease. Thyroglobulin is the marker for follicular-cell-derived cancers (PTC/FTC/oncocytic), not MTC.
items.reference: MTC: "Elevated serum calcitonin provides a marker of residual or recurrent disease."

items.question: An elderly woman with Hashimoto's thyroiditis develops a rapidly enlarging thyroid mass; biopsy shows diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. What is the appropriate initial management?
options: Avoid surgical resection; treat as lymphoma (often highly radiosensitive)
items.explanation: Thyroid lymphoma often arises on a background of Hashimoto's and is highly radiosensitive. Surgical resection should be avoided as initial therapy because it may spread otherwise-localized disease; treatment follows lymphoma guidelines (radiation ± chemotherapy).
items.reference: Thyroid Lymphoma: "Surgical resection should be avoided as initial therapy because it may spread disease that is otherwise localized to the thyroid."

items.question: Which statement about anaplastic thyroid carcinoma is correct?
options: It is poorly differentiated, aggressive, and most patients die within ~6 months
It has an excellent prognosis with surgery alone
It is the most common thyroid cancer
items.explanation: Anaplastic thyroid cancer is poorly differentiated and aggressive; most patients die within ~6 months. Because it is undifferentiated, radioiodine uptake is usually negligible. PTC (not ATC) is the most common thyroid cancer.
items.reference: ANAPLASTIC: "ATC is a poorly differentiated and aggressive cancer. The prognosis is poor, and most patients die within 6 months of diagnosis... the uptake of radioiodine is usually negligible."

items.question: For a patient with HIGH-risk or known metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer (no contraindication to mild thyrotoxicosis), what is the recommended TSH target?
options: Keep TSH in the upper normal range
items.explanation: TSH suppression is risk-tailored: low risk 0.5–2.0; intermediate 0.1–0.5; high risk or known metastatic disease <0.1 mIU/L, since residual tumor remains TSH-responsive. Suppression is relaxed as surveillance confirms remission.
items.reference: TSH SUPPRESSION: "TSH should be <0.1 mIU/L for those with known metastatic disease."

items.question: To maximize radioiodine uptake before ablation of thyroid cancer, which preparation is required?
options: Elevated TSH (>25 mIU/L, via hormone withdrawal or rhTSH) plus a low-iodine diet
items.explanation: Radioiodine uptake is NIS-mediated and TSH-stimulated, so TSH must be raised (>25 mIU/L) by thyroid hormone withdrawal or recombinant human TSH; a 1–2 week low-iodine diet depletes competing stable iodine. This is most effective after near-total thyroidectomy (minimal remnant).
items.reference: RADIOIODINE: "Radioiodine is administered after iodine depletion (patient follows a low-iodine diet for 1–2 weeks) and in the presence of elevated serum TSH levels... ideally, the serum TSH level is >25 mIU/L."

items.question: After total thyroidectomy and remnant radioablation for PTC, which is the most appropriate surveillance for the common site of recurrence?
options: Serum thyroglobulin plus neck ultrasound (more sensitive than whole-body scan for nodal recurrence)
items.explanation: Because most PTC recurrences are in cervical lymph nodes, neck ultrasound (~6 months post-ablation) is more sensitive than whole-body scanning, and serum thyroglobulin is a sensitive biochemical marker after ablation. Whole-body scanning is reserved for iodine-avid metastases or elevated Tg with negative conventional imaging.
items.reference: Surveillance: "a neck ultrasound should be performed about 6 months after thyroid ablation; ultrasound has been shown to be more sensitive than WBS in this scenario."

items.question: What is the treatment of choice for a solitary autonomously functioning ('toxic') thyroid adenoma causing thyrotoxicosis?
items.explanation: Radioiodine is usually the treatment of choice: because the autonomous nodule suppresses the rest of the gland, 131I concentrates in the nodule with minimal damage to normal tissue, correcting thyrotoxicosis in ~75% by 3 months with <10% later hypothyroidism. Lobectomy is an alternative; antithyroid drugs are not optimal long-term (no spontaneous remission).
items.reference: TREATMENT (Hyperfunctioning Solitary Nodule): "Radioiodine ablation is usually the treatment of choice. Because normal thyroid function is suppressed, 131I is concentrated in the hyperfunctioning nodule."

items.question: An elderly patient with a multinodular goiter develops thyrotoxicosis after a contrast-enhanced CT scan. What phenomenon explains this?
options: Jod-Basedow phenomenon (iodine-induced hyperthyroidism in autonomous nodules)
items.explanation: Autonomous nodules in a multinodular goiter convert an iodine load (e.g., CT contrast) into excess hormone — the Jod-Basedow phenomenon. This is why iodinated contrast and other iodine sources should be avoided in nodular goiter/autonomy.
items.reference: Nontoxic MNG TREATMENT: "the risk of inducing the Jod-Basedow effect, characterized by enhanced thyroid hormone production by autonomous nodules."

items.question: When a patient with a large goiter raises both arms above the head, the face becomes congested and suffused. What does this sign indicate?
options: Pemberton's sign — thoracic-inlet (jugular venous) obstruction by the goiter
Superior vena cava syndrome from lung cancer only
items.explanation: Pemberton's sign is facial/neck congestion when the arms are raised, as the maneuver draws a large/substernal goiter into the thoracic inlet and obstructs jugular venous return — evidence of significant compression warranting CT/MRI and flow studies.
items.reference: Clinical Manifestations: "Pemberton's sign refers to facial and neck congestion due to jugular venous obstruction when the arms are raised above the head."

items.question: A patient on amiodarone develops thyrotoxicosis. Color-flow Doppler shows DECREASED thyroid vascularity and there is no underlying thyroid abnormality. Which type is this and what is the preferred treatment?
items.explanation: Decreased vascularity in a normal gland indicates type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis — a destructive thyroiditis — best treated with glucocorticoids (prednisone 40–60 mg/d). Type 1 AIT occurs in an abnormal gland with increased vascularity and is treated with antithyroid drugs.
items.reference: Amiodarone: "Color-flow Doppler ultrasonography shows increased vascularity in type 1 AIT but decreased vascularity in type 2 AIT... Glucocorticoids... are effective in treating type 2 AIT."

items.question: A patient on amiodarone develops hypothyroidism. Besides being more common in iodine-replete regions, this is most associated with which patient factor, and how is it managed?
options: Positive TPO antibodies (women); treat with levothyroxine, usually without stopping amiodarone
items.explanation: Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism is more common in women with positive TPO antibodies (failure to escape the Wolff-Chaikoff effect). It is usually unnecessary to stop amiodarone — levothyroxine normalizes function; monitor TSH (T4 may be falsely high due to impaired T4→T3 conversion).
items.reference: Amiodarone: "amiodarone-associated hypothyroidism is more common in women and individuals with positive TPO antibodies... It is usually unnecessary to discontinue amiodarone for this side effect, because LT4 can be used to normalize thyroid function."

items.question: A 1.2 cm intrathyroidal papillary carcinoma has no nodal or distant metastases and no extrathyroidal extension. Which surgical approach is acceptable?
options: Either lobectomy or near-total thyroidectomy (bilateral surgery has not been shown to improve survival)
External beam radiotherapy instead of surgery
items.explanation: For intrathyroidal tumors 1–4 cm (T1b/T2) without metastases, either lobectomy or near-total thyroidectomy is acceptable because bilateral surgery has not been shown to improve survival. Near-total thyroidectomy is reserved for tumors >4 cm, extrathyroidal extension, or metastases (and to enable radioiodine/Tg monitoring in high-risk disease).
items.reference: Surgery: "the initial surgical procedure may be either a unilateral (lobectomy) or bilateral (near-total thyroidectomy) procedure for patients with intrathyroidal cancers >1 cm and <4 cm."

items.question: For which patient does radioiodine therapy after thyroidectomy provide the LEAST benefit?
options: A patient with a stage I, T1 (≤2 cm) tumor confined to the thyroid
A patient with large-volume lymph node metastases
An older patient with distant metastases
items.explanation: Radioiodine does not improve recurrence or survival in stage I patients with T1 (≤2 cm) intrathyroidal tumors. It is reserved for higher-risk patients (larger/aggressive tumors, vascular or extrathyroidal invasion, large-volume nodal or distant metastases).
items.reference: Indications: "Neither recurrence nor survival rates are improved in stage I patients with T1 tumors (≤2 cm) confined to the thyroid."

items.question: Which lymph node finding in papillary thyroid cancer does NOT adversely affect prognosis?
options: Gross involvement of multiple 2–3 cm nodes
Micrometastases (<2 mm) in a lymph node
items.explanation: Micrometastases (<2 mm of cancer in a node) do not affect prognosis. In contrast, gross involvement of multiple 2–3 cm nodes implies a 25–30% recurrence risk and may increase mortality in older patients.
items.reference: WDTC: "Micrometastases, defined as <2 mm of cancer in a lymph node, do not affect prognosis."

items.question: A follicular thyroid carcinoma is found to have capsular invasion ONLY (no vascular invasion). What does this imply for management?
options: It has a high risk of distant metastases and needs aggressive therapy
It has a very low risk of metastasis and lobectomy alone suffices
It will not respond to any treatment
It mandates radioiodine and TSH suppression to <0.1
items.explanation: Follicular carcinoma with capsular invasion only has a very low metastatic risk, and lobectomy alone is sufficient. Angioinvasive (vascular invasion) FTC is the aggressive form that can metastasize to bone, lung, and CNS.
items.reference: WDTC (Follicular): "Follicular carcinomas with only capsular invasion have a very low risk of metastasis, and lobectomy alone suffices."

items.question: Which genetic alteration is the most common single mutation in papillary thyroid carcinoma and a target for selective inhibitors?
items.explanation: BRAF V600E occurs in up to 60% of PTC, activating the MAPK cascade; it is a target for selective BRAF inhibitors. PAX8-PPARγ is seen in FTC; CTNNB1 mutations characterize anaplastic cancer; RET germline point mutations cause familial MTC (MEN 2).
items.reference: Genetics: "BRAF V600E mutations are a common genetic alteration, occurring in up to 60% of PTC."

items.question: Which historical exposure most strongly increases the risk of thyroid cancer?
options: Childhood (before age 18) head and neck irradiation
A single CT scan in adulthood
items.explanation: Childhood head/neck irradiation (and radioactive-iodine fallout) markedly increases thyroid cancer risk, often multicentric and at younger ages, because children are especially susceptible to radiation-induced gene rearrangements (e.g., RET/PTC). Routine adult imaging is not a comparable risk.
items.reference: Risk factors/Pathogenesis: "a history of childhood (before age 18) head or neck irradiation... Children seem more predisposed to the effects of radiation than adults."

items.question: Why is LT4 (levothyroxine) suppression therapy NOT recommended to shrink thyroid nodules in iodine-replete patients?
options: Because it reliably causes thyroid cancer
Because it does not decrease nodule size in iodine-sufficient populations and risks iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis
items.explanation: In iodine-replete populations, TSH suppression with LT4 does not shrink nodules and exposes the patient to subclinical/overt thyrotoxicosis (with bone and cardiac risks). It is therefore not used for nodule management.
items.reference: Approach/Benign lesions: "The use of LT4 to suppress serum TSH is not effective in shrinking nodules in iodine-replete populations, and therefore, LT4 suppression should not be used."

items.question: An encapsulated follicular-variant papillary lesion shows NO capsular or vascular invasion on surgical pathology. How is it now classified?
options: NIFTP — noninvasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features (no longer considered malignant)
items.explanation: This non-invasive encapsulated follicular-variant lesion is reclassified as NIFTP and is no longer considered malignant, supporting conservative management (lobectomy, no radioiodine). NIFTP can only be diagnosed on surgical histology, not FNA.
items.reference: WDTC: "a subtype previously known as the encapsulated PTC follicular variant, without capsular or angioinvasion, is no longer considered malignant and has been renamed... NIFTP."

items.question: A patient has radioiodine-refractory, progressive metastatic differentiated thyroid cancer. Which therapy is most appropriate to consider?
options: Kinase inhibitors targeting RAS/BRAF/RET/VEGFR (or mutation-directed agents)
TSH stimulation to grow the tumor
items.explanation: For radioiodine-refractory progressive disease, kinase inhibitors targeting RAS/BRAF/RET/VEGFR/angiogenesis can stabilize disease; mutation-directed agents (BRAF V600E, NTRK/RET fusion inhibitors) and checkpoint inhibitors (high tumor mutation burden) are options. Patient selection matters because of toxicity; small asymptomatic metastases may be actively surveilled.
items.reference: New Potential Therapies: "Kinase inhibitors target pathways known to be active in thyroid cancer, including the RAS, BRAF, RET, EGFR, VEGFR, and angiogenesis pathways."

items.question: For which thyroid cancer is active surveillance (rather than immediate surgery) an accepted option?
options: A 3 cm follicular carcinoma with vascular invasion
An intrathyroidal micropapillary cancer ≤1.5 cm without nodal disease, extrathyroidal extension, or risky posterior location
items.explanation: Active surveillance is an option for small (≤1.5 cm) intrathyroidal micropapillary cancers without cervical nodal metastases, extrathyroidal extension, or location near the posterior capsule/recurrent laryngeal nerve. Aggressive cancers (FTC with vascular invasion, ATC, MTC) require active treatment.
items.reference: Surgery: "Active surveillance is an option for small (up to 1.5 cm) intrathyroidal micropapillary thyroid cancers without cervical lymph node metastases, extrathyroidal extension, or location near the posterior thyroid capsule."

items.question: In an elderly patient with toxic multinodular goiter, which thyroid hormone pattern is typical?
options: Low TSH with T3 often elevated more than T4
Normal TSH with high reverse T3
items.explanation: Toxic MNG produces low TSH with a free T4 that may be normal or minimally raised, while T3 is often elevated to a greater degree than T4. The scan shows heterogeneous patchy uptake. Iodine exposure can precipitate or worsen the thyrotoxicosis.
items.reference: Toxic MNG: "The TSH level is low. The free T4 level may be normal or minimally increased; T3 is often elevated to a greater degree than T4."

items.question: Why is a pretreatment 131I diagnostic scan increasingly avoided before radioiodine ablation?
options: The 131I scanning dose can cause 'stunning' that reduces uptake of the subsequent therapeutic dose
items.explanation: A 131I scanning dose can 'stun' thyroid/tumor cells, transiently reducing their uptake of the later therapeutic dose and lowering efficacy. To avoid this, 123I is used or clinicians proceed directly to ablation, reserving 131I scanning for cases where it would change management.
items.reference: 131I Ablation: "because of concerns about radioactive 'stunning' that impairs subsequent treatment, there is a trend to avoid pretreatment scanning with 131I."

items.question: Worldwide, what is the most common cause of a diffuse nontoxic (simple) goiter?
items.explanation: Iodine deficiency is the most common cause of diffuse goiter worldwide (termed endemic goiter when >5% of a population is affected), reflecting compensatory thyroid growth to trap iodide when hormone synthesis is inefficient.
items.reference: Diffuse Nontoxic Goiter: "Worldwide, diffuse goiter is most commonly caused by iodine deficiency and is termed endemic goiter when it affects >5% of the population."

items.question: A low-risk PTC patient after thyroidectomy and ablation has a negative neck ultrasound and a basal thyroglobulin <0.2 ng/mL on levothyroxine. What does this indicate?
options: Structural recurrence risk <3% at 5 years; surveillance can be de-escalated
items.explanation: Low-risk patients with negative cervical ultrasound and basal Tg <0.2 ng/mL on LT4 have <3% structural recurrence at 5 years; follow-up can be reduced to annual TSH/Tg with periodic ultrasound, keeping TSH in the lower-normal range.
items.reference: Surveillance: "In low-risk patients who have no clinical evidence of residual disease after ablation, negative cervical sonography, and a basal Tg <0.2 ng/mL on LT4, the risk of structural recurrence is <3% at 5 years."

items.statement: A 'hot' (autonomously functioning) thyroid nodule on radionuclide scan should routinely undergo FNA because of high malignancy risk.
items.explanation: False. Hot nodules are almost never malignant, so FNA is unnecessary; management addresses the hyperthyroidism. FNA is for 'cold'/non-functioning nodules selected by ultrasound pattern and size.
items.reference: Approach: "lesions with increased uptake are almost never malignant and FNA is unnecessary."

items.statement: FNA cytology can reliably distinguish a benign follicular adenoma from a follicular carcinoma.
items.explanation: False. Their nuclear cytology is identical; follicular carcinoma is defined by capsular and/or vascular invasion seen only on histology. Follicular lesions are therefore indeterminate on FNA.
items.reference: WDTC: "the distinction between benign and malignant follicular patterned lesions is often not possible using cytology alone."

items.statement: Medullary thyroid carcinoma concentrates radioiodine and is treated with 131I.
items.explanation: False. MTC arises from C cells (not follicular cells) and does NOT take up radioiodine; management is primarily surgical, with calcitonin as the tumor marker and kinase inhibitors/external radiation for palliation.
items.reference: MTC: "Unlike tumors derived from thyroid follicular cells, these tumors do not take up radioiodine."

items.statement: The rising incidence of thyroid cancer over recent decades has been matched by a large rise in disease-specific mortality.
items.explanation: False. Incidence rose markedly but disease-specific mortality barely changed — the basis for concern about overdiagnosis of small, indolent papillary cancers.
items.reference: THYROID CANCER: "the incidence of thyroid cancer has increased... However, disease-specific mortality has only minimally increased."

items.statement: TSH suppression with levothyroxine is part of the treatment of differentiated thyroid cancer because the tumors remain TSH-responsive.
items.explanation: True. Most differentiated thyroid cancers express TSH receptors and respond to TSH, so LT4 suppression of TSH removes a growth stimulus; the target depends on recurrence risk.
items.reference: TSH SUPPRESSION: "Because most tumors are still TSH-responsive, LT4 suppression of TSH has been mainstay of thyroid cancer treatment."

items.statement: Stopping amiodarone produces an immediate improvement in amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis.
items.explanation: False. Because amiodarone has a prolonged half-life and is stored in adipose tissue, stopping it gives no immediate benefit; type 2 AIT resolves on a similar timeline whether the drug is continued or stopped. The decision to stop depends on arrhythmia severity.
items.reference: Amiodarone: "because of amiodarone's prolonged half-life and storage in adipose tissue, there is no immediate benefit from discontinuing the drug."

items.statement: Iodinated contrast can be safely given to patients with an autonomous multinodular goiter.
items.explanation: False. An iodine load can precipitate the Jod-Basedow phenomenon (iodine-induced thyrotoxicosis) in autonomous nodules, so iodinated contrast and other iodine sources should be avoided in nodular goiter.
items.reference: Nontoxic MNG TREATMENT: "Contrast agents and other iodine-containing substances should be avoided because of the risk of inducing the Jod-Basedow effect."

items.statement: Papillary thyroid cancer spreads predominantly through the lymphatic system.
items.explanation: True. PTC has a propensity for lymphatic spread (though it can metastasize hematogenously to lung/bone). In contrast, follicular carcinoma spreads hematogenously.
items.reference: WDTC: "It has a propensity to spread via the lymphatic system but can metastasize hematogenously as well."

items.statement: Initial surgical resection is the preferred treatment for primary thyroid lymphoma.
items.explanation: False. Surgical resection should be avoided as initial therapy because it may spread otherwise-localized disease; thyroid lymphoma (often DLBCL on a Hashimoto's background) is highly radiosensitive and treated as a lymphoma.
items.reference: Thyroid Lymphoma: "Surgical resection should be avoided as initial therapy because it may spread disease that is otherwise localized to the thyroid."

items.statement: Radioiodine improves survival in all patients with papillary thyroid cancer, including small intrathyroidal T1 tumors.
items.explanation: False. Radioiodine does not improve recurrence or survival in stage I T1 (≤2 cm) intrathyroidal tumors, and no benefit is shown for low-risk tumors up to 4 cm. It is reserved for higher-risk disease.
items.reference: Indications: "Neither recurrence nor survival rates are improved in stage I patients with T1 tumors (≤2 cm) confined to the thyroid."

items.statement: After total thyroidectomy and ablation, neck ultrasound is more sensitive than whole-body radioiodine scanning for detecting cervical nodal recurrence of PTC.
items.explanation: True. Because most PTC recurrences are in cervical lymph nodes, neck ultrasound is more sensitive than whole-body scanning for nodal recurrence and is performed ~6 months after ablation.
items.reference: Surveillance: "ultrasound has been shown to be more sensitive than WBS in this scenario."

items.statement: RET/PTC rearrangements were observed with increased frequency in thyroid cancers arising after the Chernobyl radiation accident.
items.explanation: True. RET rearrangements occur in ~15% of PTCs and were seen more frequently in tumors developing after Chernobyl, consistent with radiation causing chromosomal rearrangements.
items.reference: Genetics: "RET rearrangements occur in 15% of PTCs... and were observed with increased frequency in tumors developing after the Chernobyl radiation accident."

items.assertion: A hot thyroid nodule on radionuclide scan does not require FNA biopsy.
items.reason: Autonomously functioning ('hot') nodules are almost never malignant.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because hot nodules are almost never malignant, FNA is unnecessary; management targets the hyperthyroidism instead.
items.reference: Approach: "lesions with increased uptake are almost never malignant and FNA is unnecessary."

items.assertion: A follicular neoplasm on FNA requires surgical histology (or molecular testing) for a definitive diagnosis.
items.reason: Follicular carcinoma is defined by capsular or vascular invasion, which cannot be assessed on cytology.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Since malignancy depends on invasion seen only on histology, FNA cannot resolve a follicular lesion, necessitating histology or molecular testing.
items.reference: WDTC: "follicular carcinoma is diagnosed by the presence of capsular and/or vascular invasion."

items.assertion: Pheochromocytoma must be excluded before thyroid surgery in a patient with RET-mutation-associated medullary thyroid carcinoma.
items.reason: RET-mutation MTC may be part of MEN 2, which includes pheochromocytoma, and operating on an undiagnosed pheochromocytoma can cause a hypertensive crisis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. MEN 2 links MTC and pheochromocytoma; excluding pheochromocytoma preoperatively prevents a potentially fatal intraoperative hypertensive crisis.
items.reference: MTC: "Prior to surgery, pheochromocytoma should be excluded in all patients with a RET mutation."

items.assertion: Thyroid hormone withdrawal or recombinant human TSH is used before radioiodine ablation.
items.reason: TSH stimulates the sodium-iodide symporter, increasing radioiodine uptake into thyroid remnant and tumor.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Raising TSH (by withdrawal or rhTSH) upregulates NIS-mediated iodine uptake, maximizing the efficacy of radioiodine therapy.
items.reference: RADIOIODINE: "Radioiodine uptake is determined primarily by expression of the NIS and is stimulated by TSH."

items.assertion: Radioiodine treatment of a toxic adenoma causes little damage to the surrounding normal thyroid.
items.reason: The autonomous nodule suppresses TSH, so the surrounding normal thyroid takes up little radioiodine.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because the autonomous nodule suppresses pituitary TSH and the surrounding gland, radioiodine concentrates in the nodule and spares normal tissue.
items.reference: TREATMENT: "Because normal thyroid function is suppressed, 131I is concentrated in the hyperfunctioning nodule with minimal uptake and damage to normal thyroid tissue."

items.assertion: Iodinated contrast should be avoided in patients with an autonomous nodular goiter.
items.reason: An iodine load can be converted by autonomous nodules into excess thyroid hormone (Jod-Basedow effect).
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Autonomous nodules use the iodine load as substrate to overproduce hormone (Jod-Basedow), so iodinated contrast can precipitate thyrotoxicosis and is avoided.
items.reference: Nontoxic MNG TREATMENT: "the risk of inducing the Jod-Basedow effect, characterized by enhanced thyroid hormone production by autonomous nodules."

items.assertion: Type 2 amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis is treated with glucocorticoids rather than antithyroid drugs.
items.reason: Type 2 AIT is a destructive thyroiditis causing release of preformed hormone, not increased synthesis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because type 2 AIT is destructive (hormone release, not synthesis), thionamides are ineffective and glucocorticoids (anti-inflammatory) are the treatment.
items.reference: Amiodarone: "Type 2 AIT... is the result of drug-induced... destructive thyroiditis... Glucocorticoids... are effective in treating type 2 AIT."

items.assertion: Radioiodine is generally ineffective for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma.
items.reason: Anaplastic carcinoma is undifferentiated, so iodine uptake is usually negligible.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because ATC is undifferentiated, it loses the NIS-mediated iodine-trapping function, so radioiodine uptake is negligible and the therapy is generally ineffective.
items.reference: ANAPLASTIC: "Because of the undifferentiated state of these tumors, the uptake of radioiodine is usually negligible."

items.assertion: A detectable and rising serum thyroglobulin after total thyroidectomy and ablation suggests recurrent thyroid cancer.
items.reason: After removal and ablation of all normal thyroid tissue, the only remaining source of thyroglobulin is residual or recurrent tumor.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. With no normal thyroid tissue left to make Tg, any detectable/rising Tg points to residual or recurrent differentiated thyroid cancer.
items.reference: Surveillance: "Serum thyroglobulin (Tg) is a sensitive marker of residual/recurrent thyroid cancer after ablation of the residual postsurgical thyroid tissue."

items.assertion: Children exposed to neck irradiation have a higher risk of thyroid cancer than similarly exposed adults.
items.reason: The childhood thyroid is more susceptible to radiation-induced genetic rearrangements.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. The developing thyroid in children is more radiosensitive, so radiation produces more cancer-driving rearrangements (e.g., RET/PTC) than in adults.
items.reference: Pathogenesis: "Children seem more predisposed to the effects of radiation than adults."

items.subtopic: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER
items.title: Understanding the Etiology of Diffuse Non-toxic Goiter
items.content: Diffuse non-toxic goiter is characterized by uniform thyroid enlargement without nodules or hyperthyroidism. It often results from iodine deficiency, which leads to compensatory thyroid growth due to inefficient hormone synthesis.
keyPoints: Occurs in the absence of nodules and hyperthyroidism
Most common cause worldwide: iodine deficiency
Endemic goiter affects >5% of a population
TSH levels are usually normal or slightly increased
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER

items.subtopic: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER
items.title: Prevalence and Characteristics of Thyroid Nodules
items.content: Thyroid nodular disease is prevalent, with physical examination revealing nodules in 3–7% of adults. Ultrasound detection increases this prevalence to up to 50%, mostly identifying small nodules (<1 cm).
keyPoints: Physical exam reveals nodules in 3-7% of adults
Ultrasound shows nodules in up to 50% of adults
Most nodules are <1 cm in diameter
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER

items.subtopic: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER
items.title: Mechanisms of Goiter Development in Iodine Deficiency
items.content: In iodine-deficient areas, goiter results from the thyroid's compensatory efforts to produce sufficient hormone. This involves increased sensitivity to TSH or other growth pathways, with direct actions on thyroid vasculature.
keyPoints: Compensatory effort in iodine deficiency
TSH levels usually normal or slightly elevated
Direct effects of iodide on thyroid vasculature
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer | GOITER AND THYROID NODULAR DISEASE | DIFFUSE NONTOXIC (SIMPLE) GOITER

items.title: Evaluating Substernal Goiter for Obstruction
items.content: Substernal goiters can obstruct the thoracic inlet, leading to symptoms such as respiratory distress or dysphagia. Pemberton's sign is a clinical maneuver used to detect jugular venous obstruction by raising the arms above the head, which may cause facial and neck congestion if the thyroid compresses the thoracic inlet.
keyPoints: Substernal goiter can obstruct the thoracic inlet.
Pemberton's sign indicates jugular venous obstruction due to thyroid compression.
Respiratory flow measurements and CT or MRI are recommended for evaluating substernal goiters with obstructive signs or symptoms.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Interpreting Thyroid Function Tests in Goiter Patients
items.content: Thyroid function tests are essential for all patients with goiter to rule out thyrotoxicosis or hypothyroidism. In iodine deficiency, a low total T4 with normal T3 and TSH may occur due to enhanced T4 to T3 conversion. Low TSH with normal free T3 and T4 suggests thyroid autonomy or undiagnosed Graves' disease, termed subclinical thyrotoxicosis.
keyPoints: Thyroid function tests are necessary for all goiter patients.
Low total T4 with normal T3 and TSH may indicate iodine deficiency.
Low TSH with normal free T3 and T4 suggests thyroid autonomy or undiagnosed Graves' disease (subclinical thyrotoxicosis).
Treatment of subclinical thyrotoxicosis is increasingly recommended in the elderly to reduce atrial fibrillation and bone loss risk.
Low urinary iodine levels (<50 µg/L) support a diagnosis of iodine deficiency.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER
items.title: When to Consider Surgery for Diffuse Goiter
items.content: Surgery is rarely indicated for diffuse goiter but may be necessary in cases of documented tracheal compression or thoracic inlet obstruction, particularly with substernal multinodular goiters. Subtotal or near-total thyroidectomy should be performed by an experienced surgeon to minimize complications.
keyPoints: Surgery is generally not indicated for diffuse goiter.
Exceptions include documented evidence of tracheal compression or thoracic inlet obstruction.
Subtotal or near-total thyroidectomy should be performed by an experienced surgeon.
Post-surgical levothyroxine (LT4) replacement is necessary.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER

items.subtopic: Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER
items.title: Etiology and Pathogenesis of Multinodular Goiter
items.content: Multinodular goiter is more prevalent in women, increases with age, and can occur both in iodine-deficient and sufficient regions. It involves a hyperplastic response to growth factors and cytokines, often resulting in fibrosis.
keyPoints: MNG occurs in up to 12% of adults.
More common in women than men.
Increases with age.
Occurs in both iodine-deficient and sufficient regions.
Histology shows hyperplastic response, fibrosis, and possible monoclonal lesions.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER

items.subtopic: Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Multinodular Goiter
items.content: Most patients with nontoxic MNG are asymptomatic. Diagnosis involves physical examination, TSH measurement, and imaging studies like CT or MRI to assess tracheal compression, which is significant if it exceeds 70% of the tracheal diameter.
keyPoints: Most patients with nontoxic MNG are asymptomatic and euthyroid.
Pemberton's sign suggests increased pressure in the thoracic inlet.
TSH measurement excludes subclinical thyroid dysfunction.
Tracheal compression is significant if it exceeds 70% of tracheal diameter.
CT or MRI assesses anatomy, substernal extension, and tracheal narrowing.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Diffuse Nontoxic (Simple) Goiter | NONTOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER

items.subtopic: Nontoxic Multinodular Goiter | TOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER
items.title: Management of Nontoxic Multinodular Goiter (MNG)
items.content: Most nontoxic MNGs are managed conservatively. T4 suppression is rarely effective and poses a risk for thyrotoxicosis, especially with underlying autonomy or if it develops during treatment. Radioiodine therapy can reduce goiter volume and selectively ablate autonomous regions in areas where surgery is contraindicated.
keyPoints: T4 suppression rarely reduces goiter size; may cause subclinical or overt thyrotoxicosis.
Radioiodine dosage: 3.7 MBq (0.1 mCi) per gram of tissue, corrected for uptake, typically 370–1070 MBq (10–29 mCi).
Achieve a 40-50% reduction in goiter size with radioiodine.
Repeat treatment may be needed; effectiveness increased by concurrent low-dose recombinant TSH (0.1 mg IM).
Radiation-induced thyroid swelling and tracheal compression are rare; hypothyroidism is less common than after Graves' disease treatment.
Posttreatment autoimmune thyrotoxicosis occurs in up to 5% of patients.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Nontoxic Multinodular Goiter | TOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER

items.subtopic: Nontoxic Multinodular Goiter | TOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Toxic Multinodular Goiter (MNG)
items.content: Toxic MNG presents with hyperthyroidism due to functional autonomy. Clinical features include atrial fibrillation, palpitations, tachycardia, nervousness, tremor, or weight loss in elderly patients. Iodine exposure can exacerbate thyrotoxicosis. Diagnosis involves low TSH and elevated T3 levels; thyroid scan shows heterogeneous uptake.
keyPoints: Toxic MNG differs from nontoxic by functional autonomy.
Clinical presentation includes subclinical or mild overt hyperthyroidism, often in elderly patients.
Iodine exposure may precipitate thyrotoxicosis.
TSH level is low; free T4 normal/minimally increased; T3 often elevated more than T4.
Thyroid scan shows heterogeneous uptake with multiple regions of increased and decreased uptake.
Ultrasound imaging should be performed to assess discrete nodules before definitive treatment.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Nontoxic Multinodular Goiter | TOXIC MULTINODULAR GOITER

items.subtopic: Toxic Multinodular Goiter | HYPERFFUNCTIONING SOLITARY NODULE
items.title: Management of Toxic Multinodular Goiter in Elderly or Ill Patients
items.content: Antithyroid drugs are particularly useful for normalizing thyroid function in elderly or ill patients with limited life spans. This approach is preferred due to its effectiveness and safety profile in these populations.
keyPoints: Antithyroid drugs normalize thyroid function effectively in the elderly or ill patients.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Toxic Multinodular Goiter | HYPERFFUNCTIONING SOLITARY NODULE

items.subtopic: Toxic Multinodular Goiter | HYPERFFUNCTIONING SOLITARY NODULE
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyperfunctioning Solitary Thyroid Nodule (Toxic Adenoma)
items.content: A solitary, autonomously functioning thyroid nodule is known as a toxic adenoma. It is characterized by mutations in the TSH-R or Gsα genes that lead to thyrotoxicosis, typically mild and detected when nodules are >3 cm. Diagnosis involves identifying subnormal TSH levels and using a thyroid scan for focal uptake. Treatment options include radioiodine therapy, which reduces nodule mass and treats autonomy, and surgery after achieving euthyroid status.
keyPoints: Toxic adenoma is caused by mutations in the TSH-R or Gsα genes (>90% of cases).
Thyrotoxicosis is usually mild and detected when nodules are >3 cm.
Diagnosis involves subnormal TSH levels and a thyroid scan showing focal uptake.
Radioiodine therapy treats autonomy and reduces nodule mass; surgery follows euthroid status achievement.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Toxic Multinodular Goiter | HYPERFFUNCTIONING SOLITARY NODULE

items.title: Radioiodine Ablation for Hyperfunctioning Thyroid Nodules
items.content: Radioiodine ablation is the preferred treatment for hyperfunctioning thyroid nodules. It effectively suppresses normal thyroid function, concentrating $^{131}$I in the nodule and minimizing uptake by surrounding tissue.
keyPoints: Relatively large doses of radioiodine (e.g., 370–1110 MBq [10–29.9 mCi] $^{131}$I) correct thyrotoxicosis in ~75% of patients within 3 months.
Hypothyroidism occurs in <10% of those patients over the next 5 years.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Hyperfunctioning Solitary Nodule | BENIGN LESIONS

items.title: Ultrasound-Guided FNA for Thyroid Nodules
items.content: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) with ultrasound guidance is the diagnostic procedure of choice for evaluating thyroid nodules, helping to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions.
keyPoints: Benign nodules may be hyperplastic or neoplastic adenomas with macro- or microfollicular architecture.
Spongiform nodules require >50% microcystic areas by volume for diagnosis.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Hyperfunctioning Solitary Nodule | BENIGN LESIONS

items.title: TSH Suppression and Nodule Size Management in Iodine-Sufficient Populations
items.content: In iodine-sufficient populations, TSH suppression with LT4 therapy does not decrease thyroid nodule size. However, if there is relative iodine deficiency, both iodine and LT4 therapy can reduce nodule volume.
keyPoints: TSH suppression with LT4 therapy does not decrease thyroid nodule size in iodine-sufficient populations.
In cases of relative iodine deficiency, both iodine and LT4 therapy have been shown to decrease nodule volume.
If LT4 is used, TSH should be maintained at or just below the lower limit of normal but not suppressed.
Treatment should be discontinued if there's no size reduction after 6–12 months due to minimal benefits and risk of iatrogenic subclinical thyrotoxicosis.

items.title: Trends and Management in Thyroid Cancer Diagnosis
items.content: The incidence of thyroid cancer has increased, largely due to overdiagnosis of small T1 papillary cancers. Current trends focus on avoiding overdiagnosis by using evidence-based guidelines that recommend higher size thresholds for nodule FNA.
keyPoints: Incidence of thyroid cancer in the U.S. increased from 4.9 to >15 cases per 100,000 individuals over 30 years.
Increased incidence is mainly due to small T1 papillary cancers (<2 cm).
Disease-specific mortality has not significantly changed despite higher diagnosis rates.
Current trends aim to avoid overdiagnosis by limiting FNA based on sonographic risk stratification with size cutoffs.

items.title: Characteristics and Prognosis of Differentiated vs. Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer
items.content: Differentiated thyroid cancers like PTC and FTC are often curable with a good prognosis in early stages, whereas anaplastic thyroid cancer (ATC) is aggressive, poorly responsive to treatment, and has a poor prognosis.
keyPoints: Thyroid carcinoma is the most common malignancy of the endocrine system.
Differentiated tumors such as PTC or FTC are often curable with early detection.
Anaplastic thyroid cancer (ATC) is aggressive, responds poorly to treatment, and has a poor prognosis.
Prognosis worsens in older persons (>65 years), males, and those with risk factors like childhood irradiation.
