items.title: Radiation's Role in Thyroid Cancer Pathogenesis
items.content: External radiation exposure, particularly to the mediastinum, face, head, and neck regions, predisposes individuals to thyroid cancer by causing chromosomal breaks that lead to genetic rearrangements and loss of tumor-suppressor genes. This increases both benign and malignant nodule risk, often resulting in multicentric cancers and earlier onset.
keyPoints: Radiation exposure is linked to an increased risk of both benign and malignant thyroid nodules.
Radiation shifts the incidence of thyroid cancer to a younger age group.
Children are more susceptible to radiation effects than adults.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · CLASSIFICATION | ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Mutations in Thyroid Cancer
items.content: Thyroid cancers, particularly papillary thyroid carcinomas (PTCs), frequently involve mutations in the RET-RAS-BRAF signaling pathway. BRAF V600E mutations are common in up to 60% of PTCs and activate the MAPK cascade, while RAS mutations occur in ~15% of thyroid neoplasms.
keyPoints: BRAF V600E mutations occur in up to 60% of PTCs.
RAS mutations are found in approximately 15% of thyroid neoplasms.
RET rearrangements are observed in about 15% of PTCs, with increased frequency post-Chernobyl.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · CLASSIFICATION | ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 1)

items.title: Distinct Genetic Features of Anaplastic Thyroid Carcinomas
items.content: Anaplastic thyroid carcinomas (ATCs) exhibit distinct genetic alterations compared to differentiated thyroid cancers, including TERT promoter mutations in <10% of PTCs but more common in ATC, and CTNNB1 mutations found in about two-thirds of ATCs.
keyPoints: <10% of differentiated PTCs have TERT promoter mutations, whereas they are more frequent in ATC.
CTNNB1 mutations occur in approximately two-thirds of ATCs but not in PTC or FTC.
P53 mutations contribute to genetic instability and poor treatment responses in ATC.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · CLASSIFICATION | ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER
items.title: Molecular Diagnostics in Thyroid Nodules
items.content: Molecular diagnostics are increasingly used to differentiate benign from malignant thyroid nodules, especially after fine-needle aspiration (FNA), reducing unnecessary surgeries for indeterminate nodules. These diagnostic panels can also distinguish between parathyroid and medullary nodules versus thyroid follicular cell-derived lesions.
keyPoints: Molecular diagnostics help reduce the number of diagnostic surgeries for indeterminate thyroid nodules.
Diagnostic panels differentiate between benign and malignant lesions post-FNA.
Panels distinguish parathyroid, medullary, and thyroid follicular cell-derived lesions.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER
items.title: Prognostic Implications of Lymph Node Metastasis in PTC
items.content: The presence and extent of lymph node metastases significantly impact the prognosis of papillary thyroid cancer (PTC). Micrometastases (<2 mm) do not affect prognosis, while gross metastatic involvement of multiple 2- to 3-cm lymph nodes indicates a 25–30% chance of recurrence and may increase mortality in older patients.
keyPoints: Micrometastases (<2 mm) in lymph nodes do not impact PTC prognosis.
Gross metastasis involving multiple 2- to 3-cm lymph nodes suggests a 25–30% recurrence risk.
Increased mortality risk with gross lymph node involvement, especially in older patients.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER

items.subtopic: ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER
items.title: Characteristics and Prognosis of Follicular Thyroid Cancer
items.content: Follicular thyroid cancer (FTC) is more prevalent in iodine-deficient regions and accounts for about 5% of thyroid cancers in the U.S. FTC diagnosis requires histology due to similar nuclear features between benign and malignant follicular neoplasms; it is confirmed by capsular or vascular invasion. Angioinvasive FTC, which metastasizes to bone, lung, and CNS, has a poorer prognosis than PTC.
keyPoints: FTC incidence varies with iodine levels, more common in deficient regions.
Diagnosed histologically; requires evidence of capsular or vascular invasion.
Angioinvasive FTC metastasizes to bone, lung, CNS and has a poorer prognosis than PTC.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · ■ PATHOGENESIS AND GENETIC BASIS (part 2) | WELL-DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CANCER

items.subtopic: Surgery for Well-Differentiated Thyroid Cancer | TSH SUPPRESSION THERAPY
items.title: Indications and Approach to Surgery in Well-Differentiated Thyroid Cancer
items.content: For well-differentiated thyroid cancers larger than 1–1.5 cm, surgical excision is recommended. Active surveillance may be considered for smaller tumors (up to 1.5 cm) without high-risk features. Initial surgery can be unilateral or bilateral based on tumor size and absence of metastatic disease, with near-total thyroidectomy preferred in cases with extrathyroidal invasion, larger tumors (>4 cm), or high recurrence risk.
keyPoints: Surgical excision recommended for well-differentiated thyroid cancers >1–1.5 cm.
Active surveillance option for small intrathyroidal micropapillary thyroid cancers (up to 1.5 cm) without high-risk features.
Initial surgery can be unilateral or bilateral for tumors >1 cm and <4 cm in the absence of metastatic disease.
Near-total thyroidectomy preferred for extrathyroidal invasion, tumors >4 cm, or high recurrence risk.
Completion surgery recommended if aggressive pathology features are found post-lobectomy.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Surgery for Well-Differentiated Thyroid Cancer | TSH SUPPRESSION THERAPY

items.subtopic: Surgery for Well-Differentiated Thyroid Cancer | TSH SUPPRESSION THERAPY
items.title: TSH Suppression Therapy in Thyroid Cancer Management
items.content: TSH suppression therapy is crucial due to the TSH responsiveness of most thyroid tumors. The degree of suppression should be tailored based on recurrence risk, with low-risk patients maintaining TSH levels at 0.5–2.0 mIU/L, intermediate/high-risk patients at 0.1–0.5 mIU/L, and those with metastatic disease at <0.1 mIU/L.
keyPoints: TSH suppression is key due to TSH responsiveness of thyroid tumors.
Low-risk patients: maintain TSH levels at 0.5–2.0 mIU/L.
Intermediate/high-risk patients: keep TSH levels at 0.1–0.5 mIU/L.
Patients with metastatic disease: target TSH <0.1 mIU/L.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Surgery for Well-Differentiated Thyroid Cancer | TSH SUPPRESSION THERAPY

items.title: Indications for Radioiodine Therapy in Thyroid Cancer
items.content: Radioiodine therapy is not universally beneficial. It does not improve recurrence or survival rates in stage I patients with small, confined tumors (≤2 cm). However, it may reduce recurrence and increase survival in higher-risk patients, such as those with larger tumors, aggressive variants of papillary cancer, vascular invasion, extrathyroidal invasion, or large-volume lymph node metastases.
keyPoints: No benefit for stage I patients with T1 tumors (≤2 cm) confined to the thyroid.
Beneficial in higher-risk patients: larger tumors (>2 cm), aggressive variants of papillary cancer, vascular invasion, extrathyroidal invasion, large-volume lymph node metastases.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Optimizing Radioiodine Uptake in Thyroid Ablation
items.content: Radioiodine uptake is maximized by ensuring minimal remaining normal thyroid tissue and elevated serum TSH levels. This can be achieved through thyroid hormone withdrawal to elevate endogenous TSH (>25 mIU/L) or using recombinant human TSH (rhTSH). Both methods are equally effective in achieving remnant ablation.
keyPoints: Radioiodine uptake requires minimal remaining normal thyroid tissue and elevated serum TSH levels.
Elevate serum TSH >25 mIU/L through thyroid hormone withdrawal or rhTSH administration.
Both approaches (thyroid hormone withdrawal and rhTSH) are equally effective for remnant ablation.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 1)

items.title: Dosing Strategy for Radioiodine Therapy
items.content: The dose of radioiodine administered depends on the indication. Lower doses (1100 MBq or 30 mCi) are used for remnant ablation, while higher doses (up to 5500 MBq or 150 mCi) are reserved for adjuvant therapy when residual disease is suspected or present.
keyPoints: Lower dose (1100 MBq or 30 mCi) for remnant ablation.
Higher dose (up to 5500 MBq or 150 mCi) for adjuvant therapy with suspected or present residual disease.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 2) | ANAPLASTIC AND OTHER FORMS OF THYROID CANCER
items.title: Use of Kinase Inhibitors in Radioiodine-Refractory Thyroid Cancer
items.content: Kinase inhibitors target specific pathways active in thyroid cancer, such as RAS, BRAF, and RET. They can stabilize progressive metastatic disease that is refractory to radioiodine therapy, although improved survival has been demonstrated in only one study.
keyPoints: Targeted pathways: RAS, BRAF, RET, EGFR, VEGFR, angiogenesis
Stabilizes progressive metastatic disease
Improved survival shown in one study
Significant toxicities and need for ongoing therapy necessitate careful patient selection
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 2) | ANAPLASTIC AND OTHER FORMS OF THYROID CANCER

items.subtopic: RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 2) | ANAPLASTIC AND OTHER FORMS OF THYROID CANCER
items.title: Management of Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer (ATC)
items.content: Anaplastic thyroid cancer is aggressive with poor prognosis, often resulting in death within 6 months. Radioiodine uptake is usually negligible but can be used if residual uptake exists. Chemotherapy and external beam radiation therapy may offer limited benefits.
keyPoints: Poorly differentiated and aggressive
Most patients die within 6 months of diagnosis
Radioiodine uptake is usually negligible
Chemotherapy often ineffective
External beam radiation can be attempted if tumors are responsive
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · RADIOIODINE TREATMENT (part 2) | ANAPLASTIC AND OTHER FORMS OF THYROID CANCER

items.title: Understanding the Aggressiveness and Management of Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma
items.content: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) can be sporadic or familial, accounting for approximately 5% of thyroid cancers. It is more aggressive in MEN 2B compared to MEN 2A, with familial MTC being more aggressive than sporadic cases. Elevated serum calcitonin serves as a marker for residual or recurrent disease. All patients should be tested for RET mutations to facilitate genetic counseling and testing of family members.
keyPoints: MTC accounts for ~5% of thyroid cancers.
More aggressive in MEN 2B than MEN 2A, familial MTC more aggressive than sporadic.
Elevated serum calcitonin as a marker for disease recurrence.
RET mutation testing is essential for all patients with MTC.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · MEDULLARY THYROID CARCINOMA | Thyroid Nodules (part 1)

items.title: Surgical Management and Preoperative Considerations in Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma
items.content: The primary treatment for MTC is surgical. Prior to surgery, it's crucial to exclude pheochromocytoma in patients with a RET mutation due to the associated risks. Unlike follicular thyroid cancers, MTCs do not take up radioiodine; thus, external radiation and targeted kinase inhibitors may be used for palliation in advanced disease.
keyPoints: Surgical management is primary for MTC.
Exclude pheochromocytoma preoperatively in patients with RET mutations.
MTC does not take up radioiodine; consider external radiation and targeted kinase inhibitors for advanced cases.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · MEDULLARY THYROID CARCINOMA | Thyroid Nodules (part 1)

items.title: Approach to Evaluation of Thyroid Nodules
items.content: Thyroid nodules are common, with palpable ones found in ~5% of adults. Ultrasound is used to confirm the presence and characteristics of nodules detected on physical examination or imaging. The American College of Radiology (ACR) TI-RADS system classifies nodules from TR1 to TR5 based on sonographic patterns, guiding decisions about fine-needle aspiration (FNA). For instance, spongiform nodules (TR1) have a <3% chance of cancer and generally do not require FNA.
keyPoints: Palpable thyroid nodules found in ~5% of adults.
Use ultrasound to confirm palpable nodules; about 15% are not confirmed on imaging.
ACR TI-RADS classifies nodules from TR1 to TR5, guiding FNA decisions.
<3% chance of cancer for spongiform nodules (TR1), generally no FNA needed.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · MEDULLARY THYROID CARCINOMA | Thyroid Nodules (part 1)

items.title: Role of FNA Biopsy in Thyroid Nodule Evaluation
items.content: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) biopsy, particularly with ultrasound guidance, is the preferred diagnostic test for thyroid nodules. It's most effective when performed by experienced physicians and interpreted by skilled cytopathologists, especially for detecting papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC). However, distinguishing between benign and malignant follicular patterned lesions using cytology alone can be challenging due to the lack of characteristic nuclear features in follicular carcinoma.
keyPoints: FNA biopsy is best performed with ultrasound guidance by experienced physicians.
FNA yields: 50–60% benign, 5% malignant or suspicious for malignancy, 5–7% nondiagnostic, and 25–40% indeterminate.
Bethesda System provides a six-tiered classification for FNA results, aiding in uniform reporting.
NIFTP can only be diagnosed by surgical pathology and is included in malignancy estimates.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Thyroid Nodules (part 2)

items.title: Management of Indeterminate Thyroid Nodule Cytology
items.content: Indeterminate cytology results, such as AUS or FN, present a challenge in management due to varying risks of malignancy. The Bethesda System categorizes these into three indeterminate classifications with different malignancy risks. While nodules with SFM cytology generally require surgery after cervical lymph node evaluation, those with AUS and FN have a ROM ranging from 13 to 34%, often leading to diagnostic lobectomy despite many being benign.
keyPoints: SFM cytology mandates surgery post-ultrasound assessment of cervical lymph nodes.
AUS and FN nodules have a ROM of 13–34% for malignancy, with most being benign.
Diagnostic lobectomy is traditionally used for AUS and FN to obtain histopathologic diagnosis.
Molecular testing has been developed to better differentiate benign from malignant indeterminate nodules.
items.reference: Thyroid Nodular Disease and Thyroid Cancer · Thyroid Nodules (part 2)
